Supervised Learning - Foundations Project: ReCell¶

Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

Buying and selling used phones and tablets used to be something that happened on a handful of online marketplace sites. But the used and refurbished device market has grown considerably over the past decade, and a new IDC (International Data Corporation) forecast predicts that the used phone market would be worth \$52.7bn by 2023 with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 13.6% from 2018 to 2023. This growth can be attributed to an uptick in demand for used phones and tablets that offer considerable savings compared with new models.

Refurbished and used devices continue to provide cost-effective alternatives to both consumers and businesses that are looking to save money when purchasing one. There are plenty of other benefits associated with the used device market. Used and refurbished devices can be sold with warranties and can also be insured with proof of purchase. Third-party vendors/platforms, such as Verizon, Amazon, etc., provide attractive offers to customers for refurbished devices. Maximizing the longevity of devices through second-hand trade also reduces their environmental impact and helps in recycling and reducing waste. The impact of the COVID-19 outbreak may further boost this segment as consumers cut back on discretionary spending and buy phones and tablets only for immediate needs.

Objective¶

The rising potential of this comparatively under-the-radar market fuels the need for an ML-based solution to develop a dynamic pricing strategy for used and refurbished devices. ReCell, a startup aiming to tap the potential in this market, has hired you as a data scientist. They want you to analyze the data provided and build a linear regression model to predict the price of a used phone/tablet and identify factors that significantly influence it.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of used/refurbished phones and tablets. The data was collected in the year 2021. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • brand_name: Name of manufacturing brand
  • os: OS on which the device runs
  • screen_size: Size of the screen in cm
  • 4g: Whether 4G is available or not
  • 5g: Whether 5G is available or not
  • main_camera_mp: Resolution of the rear camera in megapixels
  • selfie_camera_mp: Resolution of the front camera in megapixels
  • int_memory: Amount of internal memory (ROM) in GB
  • ram: Amount of RAM in GB
  • battery: Energy capacity of the device battery in mAh
  • weight: Weight of the device in grams
  • release_year: Year when the device model was released
  • days_used: Number of days the used/refurbished device has been used
  • normalized_new_price: Normalized price of a new device of the same model in euros
  • normalized_used_price: Normalized price of the used/refurbished device in euros

Importing necessary libraries¶

In [99]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Libraries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set()

# split the data into train and test
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# to build linear regression_model
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

# to check model performance
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score

# to build linear regression_model using statsmodels
import statsmodels.api as sm

# to compute VIF
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

Data Overview¶

The overview and steps of any dataset are to:

  • Observing the first few rows of the dataset to check whether it has been loaded correctly or not.
  • Getting information about the number of rows and columns in the dataset.
  • Identifying the data types of the columns to ensure data is stored in the preferred format and the value of each property is as expected.
  • Checking the statistical summary of the dataset to get an overview of the numerical columns of the data

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Questions:

  1. What does the distribution of normalized used device prices look like?
  2. What percentage of the used device market is dominated by Android devices?
  3. The amount of RAM is important for the smooth functioning of a device. How does the amount of RAM vary with the brand?
  4. A large battery often increases a device's weight, making it feel uncomfortable in the hands. How does the weight vary for phones and tablets offering large batteries (more than 4500 mAh)?
  5. Bigger screens are desirable for entertainment purposes as they offer a better viewing experience. How many phones and tablets are available across different brands with a screen size larger than 6 inches?
  6. A lot of devices nowadays offer great selfie cameras, allowing us to capture our favorite moments with loved ones. What is the distribution of devices offering greater than 8MP selfie cameras across brands?
  7. Which attributes are highly correlated with the normalized price of a used device?

Questions 1 and 2 indeed pertain to univariate analysis:

1.What does the distribution of normalized used device prices look like?

2.What percentage of the used device market is dominated by Android devices?

Univariate Analysis¶

In [ ]:
# function to plot a boxplot and a histogram along the same scale.


def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
    kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram
In [ ]:
# function to create labeled barplots


def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
    """
    Barplot with percentage at the top

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
    n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all levels)
    """

    total = len(data[feature])  # length of the column
    count = data[feature].nunique()
    if n is None:
        plt.figure(figsize=(count + 2, 6))
    else:
        plt.figure(figsize=(n + 2, 6))

    plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
    ax = sns.countplot(
        data=data,
        x=feature,
        order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n],
    )

    for p in ax.patches:
        if perc == True:
            label = "{:.1f}%".format(
                100 * p.get_height() / total
            )  # percentage of each class of the category
        else:
            label = p.get_height()  # count of each level of the category

        x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2  # width of the plot
        y = p.get_height()  # height of the plot

        ax.annotate(
            label,
            (x, y),
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            size=12,
            xytext=(0, 5),
            textcoords="offset points",
        )  # annotate the percentage

    plt.show()  # show the plot

In [ ]:
labeled_barplot(df, "release_year", perc=True)

Observations from Univariate Analysis¶

Numerical Features (Distributions from Histogram and Boxplot):

normalized_used_price: It appears to be roughly symmetric with a slight left skew on the distribution. Mostly, used device prices fall between 4 and 5 (from normalized units), at a peak around the median of 4.4 and a mean of 4.36.

normalized_new_price: Just like used prices, new device prices are also, to some degree, symmetric but with a slight left skew. Most of the new device prices are between 4.8 and 5.8 (from normalized units), with a median around 5.25 and a mean of 5.23.

screen_size: It appears the screen sizes shows a concentration between 12 cm and 16 cm from the distribution, with a median of 12.83 cm. There are some outliers with much larger screen sizes, coming from the tablets.

main_camera_mp: It appears the main camera megapixel count is highly skewed to the right, with a large number of devices having 5 MP, 8 MP, or 13 MP cameras. The median is 8 MP and the mean is 9.46 MP. There are a few devices with very high MP counts, which are creating a long tail.

selfie_camera_mp: It ppears to also be right-skewed, with many devices having 2 MP, 5 MP, or 8 MP front cameras. The median is 5 MP and the mean is 6.55 MP, with outliers up to 32 MP.

int_memory: Internal memory is heavily skewed to the right, with 16 GB, 32 GB, and 64 GB being common. The median is 32 GB, but the mean is 54.57 GB due to devices with much larger memory (up to 1024 GB).

ram: The RAM distribution appears to concentrate around 4 GB, which is also the median and mode. There are equally some devices with lower RAM and a few with higher RAM (up to 12 GB), but 4 GB is mostly common.

weight: Generally, weight distribution is concentrated between 140g and 200g, with a median of 160g. Indeed, it appears to be right skew, indicating some heavier devices, mainly tablets.

battery: The Battery capacity is right-skewed, with a significant number of devices around 3000 mAh and 4000 mAh. The median is 3000 mAh and the mean is 3133.4 mAh, with a range up to 9720 mAh.

days_used: The number of days a device has been used shows a degree of normal distribution but with a left skew. Most of the devices have been used for 500-900 days, with the median around 690 days and mean of 674 days.

Categorical Features (Distributions from Bar Plots):

brand_name: Realme, Huawei, Samsung, and Xiaomi, are the most dominant brands in the dataset, these devices account for a significant portion of the devices. Market shares for many other brands have smaller

os: Overwhelmingly, Android devices dominate the market and representing over 90% of the devices in the dataset. iOS devices make up a small percentage.

4g: Connectivity of 4G is supported by over 98% of almost all the devices in the dataset.

5g: Around 30%, a substantial portion, of the devices in the dataset is supported by 5G, showing a growing trend towards newer network technologies.

release_year: Primarily, the dataset contains devices released between 2014 and 2020, with a notable increases between 2019 and 2020, indicating newer models in the used device market.

Bivariate Analysis¶

Questions 3 through 7 indeed pertain to bivariate analysis

Perform a bivariate analysis on the df DataFrame to:

  1. Visualize the distribution of RAM across different device brands using a box or violin plot.
  2. Filter for devices with a battery larger than 4500 mAh and visualize the distribution of their weight using a histogram and boxplot.
  3. Count and visualize with a bar plot the number of devices with a screen size larger than 15.24 cm (6 inches) per brand.
  4. Filter for devices with a selfie camera greater than 8MP and visualize their distribution across brands using a bar plot.
  5. Calculate and visualize with a heatmap the correlation matrix of numerical features with 'normalized_used_price'. Finally, summarize the key insights derived from these analyses to answer the posed questions about the data.

To visualize the distribution of RAM across different device brands, using a box.

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 7))
sns.boxplot(x='brand_name', y='ram', data=df);
plt.title('Distribution of RAM across Brands', fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel('Brand Name', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('RAM (GB)', fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()

To visualize the distribution of weight for devices with a battery larger than 4500 mAh, first, I will filter the DataFrame, and then use the histogram_boxplot function to display both a histogram and a boxplot of the 'weight' column.

In [ ]:
df_large_battery = df[df['battery'] > 4500]
histogram_boxplot(df_large_battery, 'weight', kde=True)

To count and visualize the number of devices with a screen size larger than 15.24 cm (6 inches) per brand, first, I will filter the DataFrame based on screen size, then count the occurrences of each brand, and finally use the labeled_barplot function to visualize this data.

In [ ]:
df_large_screen = df[df['screen_size'] > 15.24]
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 7))
labeled_barplot(df_large_screen, 'brand_name', perc=False, n=None)
plt.title('Number of Devices with Screen Size > 15.24 cm per Brand', fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel('Brand Name', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Number of Devices', fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()

<Figure size 1500x700 with 0 Axes>

To filter for devices with a selfie camera greater than 8MP and visualize their distribution across brands, first, I will filter the DataFrame based on the selfie camera resolution and then use the labeled_barplot function to display the count of these devices for each brand.

In [ ]:
df_high_selfie_camera = df[df['selfie_camera_mp'] > 8]
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 7))
labeled_barplot(df_high_selfie_camera, 'brand_name', perc=False, n=None)
plt.title('Number of Devices with Selfie Camera > 8MP per Brand', fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel('Brand Name', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Number of Devices', fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()

<Figure size 1500x700 with 0 Axes>

To visualize the correlation matrix of numerical features with 'normalized_used_price', first, I will select all numerical columns from the DataFrame, calculate their correlation, and then use seaborn's heatmap to display the correlations, focusing on the target variable 'normalized_used_price'.

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))
sns.heatmap(df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).corr()[['normalized_used_price']].sort_values(by='normalized_used_price', ascending=False), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f");
plt.title('Correlation Matrix with Normalized Used Price', fontsize=15)
plt.show()

Observation from Bivariate Analysis:¶

  1. RAM Distribution across Brands: The box plot shows significant variation for RAM distribution across the brands. Some brands like Samsung, Apple, and OnePlus tend to have more consistent distributions with higher RAM capacities while others like Oppo and Xiaomi show a wider range, including devices with lower RAM.

  2. Weight Distribution for Devices with Large Batteries: Apparently, For devices with batteries larger than 4500 mAh, the histogram and boxplot of weight shows that most of these devices fall within a certain weight range (e.g., 180-250 grams), but there are also higher outliers. This indicates that larger batteries contribute to increased device weight, moreover, other components and design also play a major role.

  3. Devices with Large Screens (> 15.24 cm) per Brand: The bar plot shows that brands like Huawei, Samsung, and Apple have a substantial number of devices with screen sizes greater than 15.24 cm (6 inches). This reveals that these brands cater more to the demand for larger screen devices for productivity and entertainment.

  4. Devices with High Selfie Cameras (> 8MP) per Brand: The distribution of devices with selfie cameras greater than 8MP indicates that many brands, including Huawei,Samsung, and Xiaomi offer a good number of devices with high-resolution front cameras. This reflects the essence of selfie cameras in consumer preferences.

  5. Correlation with Normalized Used Price: The heatmap of correlations with 'normalized_used_price' highlights which numerical features have the strongest relationship with the used price. Features such as normalized_new_price, int_memory, and ram, show a strong positive correlation, while days_used might have a negative correlation. Other features such as selfie_camera_mp, main_camera_mp, and screen_size will also show their respective strengths of correlation, revealing their influence on the used device's price.

Data Preprocessing¶

  • Missing value treatment
  • Feature engineering (if needed)
  • Outlier detection and treatment (if needed)
  • Preparing data for modeling
  • Any other preprocessing steps (if needed)

I will start by addressing the missing values in the dataset. First, I will impute the missing values for numerical features like main_camera_mp, selfie_camera_mp, int_memory, ram, battery, and weight using the median, because it is robust to outliers, and these columns were shown to have skewed distributions in the univariate analysis. This will prepare the data for subsequent preprocessing steps.

Handle Missing Values¶

Impute missing values in specified numerical columns using their medians.

To impute missing values for 'main_camera_mp', 'selfie_camera_mp', 'int_memory', 'ram', 'battery', and 'weight' columns with their respective medians, I will calculate the median for each column and then use the fillna() method. After imputation, I will verify that all missing values have been addressed.

In [ ]:
for col in ['main_camera_mp', 'selfie_camera_mp', 'int_memory', 'ram', 'battery', 'weight']:
    median_val = df[col].median()
    df[col] = df[col].fillna(median_val)

# Verifying that there are no more missing values
print("Missing values after imputation:")
print(df.isnull().sum())

Missing values after imputation:
brand_name               0
os                       0
screen_size              0
4g                       0
5g                       0
main_camera_mp           0
selfie_camera_mp         0
int_memory               0
ram                      0
battery                  0
weight                   0
release_year             0
days_used                0
normalized_used_price    0
normalized_new_price     0
dtype: int64

Feature Engineering (if needed)¶

First, I will create a new feature 'age_of_model' by subtracting 'release_year' from 2021, and then drop the original 'release_year' column as its information is now encapsulated in the new feature. This directly addresses the first and third instructions of the subtask.

In [ ]:
# Check if 'release_year' column exists before performing operations
if 'release_year' in df.columns:
    df['age_of_model'] = 2021 - df['release_year']
    df.drop('release_year', axis=1, inplace=True)
    print("New feature 'age_of_model' created and 'release_year' dropped.")
    print(df[['age_of_model']].head())
else:
    # If 'release_year' is already dropped, 'age_of_model' might already exist or this step is redundant.
    # We'll check for 'age_of_model' existence and print its head if present.
    if 'age_of_model' in df.columns:
        print("'release_year' column not found, but 'age_of_model' already exists. Skipping feature engineering step.")
        print(df[['age_of_model']].head())
    else:
        print("Error: 'release_year' column not found and 'age_of_model' was not created.")

New feature 'age_of_model' created and 'release_year' dropped.
   age_of_model
0             1
1             1
2             1
3             1
4             1

Evaluation of Other Feature Engineering Opportunities¶

Beyond age_of_model, several other features could potentially be engineered to enrich the dataset and improve model performance. These include:

  • Camera Quality Score: A combined score from main_camera_mp and selfie_camera_mp (e.g., sum or weighted average) could represent overall camera capability.
  • Screen Density (PPI): If screen resolution data were available, combining it with screen_size could yield pixels per inch, a key indicator of display quality.
  • Performance Index: A combined feature from ram and int_memory could serve as a proxy for the device's overall performance capacity.
  • Battery Density: Dividing battery by weight could indicate battery efficiency relative to device size, which might influence user perception and price.
  • Connectivity Feature: Combining 4g and 5g into a single ordinal feature (e.g., 'no_4g/5g', '4g_only', '4g_and_5g') could simplify connectivity representation.

For this foundational project, the age_of_model feature is a clear and impactful addition. More complex feature engineering would typically be explored in subsequent iterative model refinement steps, after establishing a baseline with simpler features.

Outlier Detection and Treatment (if needed)¶

First, I will re-examine the box plots for all numerical features to visually identify potential outliers. I will start with 'screen_size' using the histogram_boxplot function.

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(df, 'screen_size', kde=True)

I will continue to re-examine the box plots for all numerical features to visually identify potential outliers. Next is 'main_camera_mp' using the histogram_boxplot function.

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(df, 'main_camera_mp', kde=True)

I will continue to re-examine the box plots for all numerical features to visually identify potential outliers. Next will be 'selfie_camera_mp' using the histogram_boxplot function.

histogram_boxplot(df, 'selfie_camera_mp', kde=True)

I will re-examine the box plot for the newly created numerical feature 'age_of_model' to visually identify potential outliers.

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(df, 'age_of_model', kde=True)

Outlier Analysis Summary and Treatment Strategy¶

Based on the re-examination of box plots for all numerical features, the following observations and treatment strategies are proposed:

1. screen_size: No outlier treatment is necessary. It shows that the dataset includes both phones and tablets, these larger screen sizes (e.g., 25.5 cm, 30.71 cm) are representations of tablets. However, it is an indication of diverse device types in the dataset.

2. main_camera_mp: This feature shows that there are significant number of outliers on the higher end. While most devices cluster around 5-13 MP, there are values up to 48 MP. These higher values represent premium devices with advanced camera technology.However, there is no outlier treatment that is necessary.

3. selfie_camera_mp: This feature shows outliers on the higher end (up to 32 MP). These values correspond to devices with a high-resolution front cameras. These can be legitimate variations in the device specifications and no outlier treatment is necessary.

4. int_memory: This feature shows numerous outliers on the higher side, with values exceeding significantly beyond the range (e.g., 1024 GB). While 1TB (1024GB) internal memory is available in modern premium devices, the extreme values are valid high-end specifications and no outlier treatment is necessary.

5. ram: The ram shows some outliers on both the lower and higher sides. While 4GB RAM is most common, some devices have 2GB, and others have 6GB, 8GB, or even 12GB. These variations are legitimate specifications found in the market and no outlier treatment is necessary.

6. battery: Battery capacity shows outliers on the higher side (up to 9720 mAh). Very large batteries Devices, especially tablets, exist. These high values are accurate representations of specific device models and no outlier treatment is necessary.

7. weight: Primarily, the weight shows outliers on the higher side (up to 855g).It is expected as heavier devices sometimes correspond to larger screen sizes (tablets) or devices with larger batteries. These are legitimate physical characteristics and no outlier treatment is necessary.

8. days_used: This feature appears to have a relatively normal distribution with few extreme outliers. The range of days used is plausible for used devices and no outlier treatment is necessary.

9. normalized_used_price: This normalized_used_price shows a few outliers on both the lower and higher ends. However, these represent cheap or expensive used devices. These are prices and could be legitimate, as we are trying to predict this variable, we should leave them, as they contribute to the full range of price prediction. Therefore, no outlier treatment is necessary.

10. normalized_new_price: This feature also shows some outliers on both ends and it is similia to used price. These is correspond to budget or premium devices. It's important to retain them to capture the full spectrum of new device pricing. Therefore, no outlier treatment is necessary.

11. age_of_model: The age_of_model shows outliers on the higher end, indicating older models (up to 8 years old). These are legitimate specifications data points for older devices in the used market. These older devices can still be sold and bought and no outlier treatment is necessary.

Prepare Data for Modeling¶

Perform necessary transformations, such as one-hot encoding for categorical variables and scaling numerical features, to prepare the data for linear regression modeling.

First, I will identify the categorical columns and apply one-hot encoding to them using pd.get_dummies(). This step converts categorical variables into a format suitable for linear regression models and addresses the first two instructions.

In [ ]:
categorical_cols = ['brand_name', 'os', '4g', '5g']
df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=categorical_cols, drop_first=True)

print("DataFrame after one-hot encoding categorical variables:")
print(df_encoded.head())
print(f"New shape of DataFrame: {df_encoded.shape}")

DataFrame after one-hot encoding categorical variables:

   weight  days_used  normalized_used_price  normalized_new_price  ...  \

4           False      False       False   False    True   False  

[5 rows x 49 columns]
New shape of DataFrame: (3454, 49)

Now, I will identify the numerical columns from the df_encoded DataFrame, excluding the target variable 'normalized_used_price' and the new 'age_of_model' feature, and then apply StandardScaler to scale these features.

In [ ]:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Identify numerical columns to scale (excluding target)
numerical_cols = df_encoded.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()
numerical_cols.remove('normalized_used_price')

# Ensure 'age_of_model' is treated as numerical for scaling if it's already there
# The imputation was intended for 'df' but needs to be explicitly applied to 'df_encoded' before scaling.
# Columns identified to have missing values from df.info() were: main_camera_mp, selfie_camera_mp, int_memory, ram, battery, weight

# Re-apply median imputation to numerical columns that still have NaNs in df_encoded
for col in ['main_camera_mp', 'selfie_camera_mp', 'int_memory', 'ram', 'battery', 'weight']:
    if col in df_encoded.columns and df_encoded[col].isnull().any():
        median_val = df_encoded[col].median()
        df_encoded[col] = df_encoded[col].fillna(median_val)

# Initialize StandardScaler
scaler = StandardScaler()

# Apply scaling to numerical features
df_encoded[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(df_encoded[numerical_cols])

print("Numerical features scaled successfully.")
print(df_encoded[numerical_cols].head())

Numerical features scaled successfully.

All numerical features have been scaled and categorical features have been one-hot encoded and integrated into df_encoded. The target variable normalized_used_price is also present in df_encoded. I will now print the head of the final DataFrame to confirm its structure.

Final shape of DataFrame: (3454, 49)

Observation on Data Analysis¶

  • Missing Value Imputation: Missing values in numerical columns (main_camera_mp, selfie_camera_mp, int_memory, ram, battery, weight) were successfully imputed using their respective medians. After imputation, no missing values remained in the DataFrame.
  • Feature Engineering: A new feature, age_of_model, was created by subtracting the release_year from 2021. The original release_year column was subsequently dropped. The new feature captures the age of the device.
  • Outlier Treatment Decision: After visualizing all numerical features and analyzing potential outliers, it was determined that most identified "outliers" were legitimate extreme values reflecting the diversity of devices in the market (e.g., larger screens for tablets, high-megapixel cameras, high internal memory, large battery capacities, older models, and varied price ranges). Therefore, no outlier treatment was performed to avoid losing valuable information.
  • Data Transformation for Modeling:
    • Categorical features (brand_name, os, 4g, 5g) were one-hot encoded using drop_first=True to handle nominal data and prevent multicollinearity. This increased the DataFrame's column count to 49.
    • All numerical features (excluding the target variable normalized_used_price) were scaled using StandardScaler, ensuring they have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1, which is beneficial for linear regression models.

Insights¶

  • The dataset is now thoroughly preprocessed, with missing values handled, a new relevant feature engineered, outliers assessed, and data transformed for machine learning, making it ready for the model building phase.

EDA¶

  • It is a good idea to explore the data once again after manipulating it.

Display the first few rows, data types, non-null counts, and statistical summary of the df_encoded DataFrame to confirm the effect of preprocessing (one-hot encoding and scaling).

Review Numerical Feature Distributions¶

Visually inspecting the distributions of key numerical features, including the target variable normalized_used_price and the new age_of_model, from the df_encoded DataFrame using histograms and boxplots. This helps us to ensure scaling has not altered their fundamental shape and to understand the distribution of the engineered feature.

To visually inspect the distribution of the scaled numerical features, including the engineered 'age_of_model', and ensure scaling has not altered their fundamental shape, I will iterate through the relevant numerical columns in df_encoded and use the histogram_boxplot function.

In [ ]:
for col in ['screen_size', 'main_camera_mp', 'selfie_camera_mp', 'int_memory', 'ram', 'battery', 'weight', 'days_used', 'normalized_new_price', 'age_of_model']:
    print(f"Distribution of {col}:")
    histogram_boxplot(df_encoded, col, kde=True)

To complete the visualization inspection of numerical feature distributions, I will generate the histogram and boxplot for the target variable normalized_used_price from the df_encoded DataFrame.

In [ ]:
print("Distribution of normalized_used_price:")
histogram_boxplot(df_encoded, 'normalized_used_price', kde=True)

Distribution of normalized_used_price:

Re-evaluate Correlations¶

Re-calculating and visualizing the correlation matrix (heatmap) of all numerical features (including the scaled ones and age_of_model) with normalized_used_price in the df_encoded DataFrame. It will confirm strong relationships and observe any changes post-scaling.

Visualizing the correlations of all numerical features (including scaled and engineered ones) with the target variable, I will calculate the correlation matrix and display it as a heatmap, focusing on 'normalized_used_price'.

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))
sns.heatmap(df_encoded.select_dtypes(include=np.number).corr()[['normalized_used_price']].sort_values(by='normalized_used_price', ascending=False), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f");
plt.title('Correlation Matrix with Normalized Used Price (Post-Preprocessing)', fontsize=15)
plt.show()

Observations from the post-preprocessing EDA, noting how the transformations have affected the data's distributions and structure, to confirm readiness for model building.

Data Analysis¶

  • The df_encoded DataFrame, containing 3454 entries and 49 columns, is free of missing values, with all columns showing 3454 non-null entries.

  • Numerical features (e.g., screen_size, main_camera_mp, ram, battery, weight, days_used, age_of_model, normalized_new_price) have been successfully standardized, resulting in means very close to 0 and standard deviations very close to 1.

  • Categorical features have been effectively one-hot encoded into 38 boolean columns, confirming the transformation.

  • The target variable, normalized_used_price, has a mean of approximately 4.36 and a standard deviation of approximately 0.58.

  • Visualizations of numerical feature distributions (histograms and boxplots) were generated for all specified columns, including normalized_used_price and the engineered age_of_model, allowing for a visual assessment of their shapes post-scaling.

  • The correlation matrix with normalized_used_price for all numerical features, including scaled and engineered ones, was re-calculated and visualized as a heatmap, confirming relationships after preprocessing.

  • The data is confirmed to be clean, scaled, and encoded appropriately, showing it is now ready for model building.

  • Further analysis could involve selecting specific features based on their correlation with normalized_used_price for initial model training or feature engineering, especially for those revealing strong relationships.

Model Building - Linear Regression¶

In [98]:
# Separating the independent variables (X) and the dependent variable (y)
X = df_encoded.drop('normalized_used_price', axis=1)
y = df_encoded['normalized_used_price']

# Convert boolean columns to integer (0 or 1) for statsmodels compatibility
for col in X.select_dtypes(include='bool').columns:
    X[col] = X[col].astype(int)

# Splitting the data into training and testing sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Adding a constant to the independent variables for statsmodels.OLS
X_train_sm = sm.add_constant(X_train)

olsmodel1 = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train_sm).fit()
print(olsmodel1.summary())

                              OLS Regression Results                             
=================================================================================

Model:                               OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.845
Method:                    Least Squares   F-statistic:                     276.3
Date:                   Sun, 07 Dec 2025   Prob (F-statistic):               0.00
Time:                           21:08:47   Log-Likelihood:                 122.14
No. Observations:                   2417   AIC:                            -146.3
Df Residuals:                       2368   BIC:                             137.4
Df Model:                             48                                         
Covariance Type:               nonrobust                                         
=========================================================================================
                            coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const                     4.3873      0.043    101.768      0.000       4.303       4.472

Notes:

Model Performance Check¶

Let's check the performance of the model using different metrics.

  • I will be using metric functions defined in sklearn for RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$.
  • I will define a function to calculate MAPE and adjusted $R^2$.
  • I will create a function which will print out all the above metrics in one go.
In [ ]:
# function to compute adjusted R-squared
def adj_r2_score(predictors, targets, predictions):
    r2 = r2_score(targets, predictions)
    n = predictors.shape[0]
    k = predictors.shape[1]
    return 1 - ((1 - r2) * (n - 1) / (n - k - 1))


# function to compute MAPE
def mape_score(targets, predictions):
    return np.mean(np.abs(targets - predictions) / targets) * 100


# function to compute different metrics to check performance of a regression model
def model_performance_regression(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check regression model performance

    model: regressor
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    r2 = r2_score(target, pred)  # to compute R-squared
    adjr2 = adj_r2_score(predictors, target, pred)  # to compute adjusted R-squared
    rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(target, pred))  # to compute RMSE
    mae = mean_absolute_error(target, pred)  # to compute MAE
    mape = mape_score(target, pred)  # to compute MAPE

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "RMSE": rmse,
            "MAE": mae,
            "R-squared": r2,
            "Adj. R-squared": adjr2,
            "MAPE": mape,
        },
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf
In [ ]:
# checking model performance on train set (seen 70% data)
print("Training Performance\n")
# The olsmodel1 was trained with X_train_sm, so we need to pass X_train_sm for prediction as well.
olsmodel1_train_perf = model_performance_regression(olsmodel1, X_train_sm, y_train)
olsmodel1_train_perf

Training Performance
Out[ ]:
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
0 0.230046 0.178413 0.848511 0.845374 4.291322
In [ ]:
# checking model performance on test set (seen 30% data)
print("Test Performance\n")
# Add constant to X_test to match the training data (X_train_sm) used for olsmodel1
X_test_sm = sm.add_constant(X_test)
olsmodel1_test_perf = model_performance_regression(olsmodel1, X_test_sm, y_test)
olsmodel1_test_perf

Test Performance
Out[ ]:
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
0 0.238234 0.188312 0.833336 0.825062 4.50779

Checking Linear Regression Assumptions¶

  • In order to make statistical inferences from a linear regression model, it is important to ensure that the assumptions of linear regression are satisfied.

I will be checking the following Linear Regression assumptions:

  1. No Multicollinearity

  2. Linearity of variables

  3. Independence of error terms

  4. Normality of error terms

  5. No Heteroscedasticity

TEST FOR MULTICOLLINEARITY¶

I will test for multicollinearity using VIF.

General Rule of thumb:

  • If VIF is 1 then there is no correlation between the $k$th predictor and the remaining predictor variables.

  • If VIF exceeds 5 or is close to exceeding 5, we say there is moderate multicollinearity.

  • If VIF is 10 or exceeding 10, it shows signs of high multicollinearity

Let's define a function to check VIF.

In [ ]:
#Let's define a function to check VIF.

def checking_vif(predictors):
    vif = pd.DataFrame()
    vif["feature"] = predictors.columns

    # calculating VIF for each feature
    vif["VIF"] = [
        variance_inflation_factor(predictors.values, i)
        for i in range(len(predictors.columns))
    ]
    return vif
In [ ]:
vif_results = checking_vif(X_train_sm)
print(vif_results.sort_values(by='VIF', ascending=False).head(10))

               feature        VIF
0                const  83.163241
12    brand_name_Apple  11.192704
34   brand_name_Others  10.834358
46              os_iOS  10.036219
1          screen_size   8.264079
37  brand_name_Samsung   8.012882
7               weight   6.405860
21   brand_name_Huawei   6.397788
24       brand_name_LG   5.353108
10        age_of_model   4.860595

Removing Multicollinearity (if needed)¶

To remove multicollinearity

  1. I will drop every column one by one that has a VIF score greater than 5.
  2. I will Look at the adjusted R-squared and RMSE of all these models.
  3. I will drop the variable that makes the least change in adjusted R-squared.
  4. I will be checking the VIF scores again.
  5. I will Continue till I get all VIF scores under 5.

Let's define a function that will help us do this.

In [ ]:
def treating_multicollinearity(predictors, target, high_vif_columns):
    """
    Checking the effect of dropping the columns showing high multicollinearity
    on model performance (adj. R-squared and RMSE)

    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    high_vif_columns: columns having high VIF
    """
    # empty lists to store adj. R-squared and RMSE values
    adj_r2 = []
    rmse = []

    # build ols models by dropping one of the high VIF columns at a time
    # store the adjusted R-squared and RMSE in the lists defined previously
    for cols in high_vif_columns:
        # defining the new train set
        train = predictors.loc[:, ~predictors.columns.str.startswith(cols)]

        # create the model
        olsmodel = sm.OLS(target, train).fit()

        # adding adj. R-squared and RMSE to the lists
        adj_r2.append(olsmodel.rsquared_adj)
        rmse.append(np.sqrt(olsmodel.mse_resid))

    # creating a dataframe for the results
    temp = pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "col": high_vif_columns,
            "Adj. R-squared after_dropping col": adj_r2,
            "RMSE after dropping col": rmse,
        }
    ).sort_values(by="Adj. R-squared after_dropping col", ascending=False)
    temp.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

    return temp
In [ ]:
x_train2 = X_train_sm.copy() # Initialize x_train2 with the full set of predictors
col_list = ['screen_size', 'weight']

res = treating_multicollinearity(x_train2, y_train, col_list)
res
Out[ ]:
col Adj. R-squared after_dropping col RMSE after dropping col
0 weight 0.843254 0.234052
1 screen_size 0.841083 0.235667
In [ ]:
col_to_drop = 'weight'
x_train2 = x_train2.loc[:, ~x_train2.columns.str.startswith(col_to_drop)]
X_test_sm_temp = sm.add_constant(X_test)
x_test2 = X_test_sm_temp.loc[:, ~X_test_sm_temp.columns.str.startswith(col_to_drop)]

# Check VIF now
vif = checking_vif(x_train2)
print("VIF after dropping ", col_to_drop)
vif

Dropping high p-value variables (if needed)¶

  • I will be dropping the predictor variables having a p-value greater than 0.05 as they do not significantly impact the target variable.
  • But sometimes p-values change after dropping a variable. So, I'll not drop all variables at once.
  • Instead, I will do this following:
    • Build a model, check the p-values of the variables, and drop the column with the highest p-value.
    • Create a new model without the dropped feature, check the p-values of the variables, and drop the column with the highest p-value.
    • Repeat the above two steps till there are no columns with p-value > 0.05.

The above process can also be done manually by picking one variable at a time that has a high p-value, dropping it, and building a model again. But that might be a little tedious and using a loop will be more efficient.

In [ ]:
# initial list of columns
predictors = x_train2.copy()
cols = predictors.columns.tolist()

# setting an initial max p-value
max_p_value = 1

while len(cols) > 0:
    # defining the train set
    x_train_aux = predictors[cols]

    # fitting the model
    model = sm.OLS(y_train, x_train_aux).fit()

    # getting the p-values and the maximum p-value
    p_values = model.pvalues
    max_p_value = max(p_values)

    # name of the variable with maximum p-value
    feature_with_p_max = p_values.idxmax()

    if max_p_value > 0.05:
        cols.remove(feature_with_p_max)
    else:
        break

selected_features = cols
print(selected_features)

['const', 'screen_size', 'main_camera_mp', 'selfie_camera_mp', 'int_memory', 'ram', 'normalized_new_price', 'age_of_model', 'brand_name_Celkon', '4g_yes']
In [ ]:
x_train3 = x_train2[selected_features]
x_test3 = x_test2[selected_features]
In [ ]:
olsmodel2 = sm.OLS(y_train, x_train3).fit()
print(olsmodel2.summary())

print("Test Performance\n")
olsmodel2_test_perf = model_performance_regression(olsmodel2, x_test3, y_test)
olsmodel2_test_perf

RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
0 0.241178 0.190888 0.829191 0.827526 4.568832

TEST FOR LINEARITY AND INDEPENDENCE¶

  • I will test for linearity and independence by making a plot of fitted values vs residuals and checking for patterns.
  • If there is no pattern, then I will say the model is linear and residuals are independent.
  • Otherwise, the model is showing signs of non-linearity and residuals are not independent.
In [ ]:
# let us create a dataframe with actual, fitted and residual values
df_pred = pd.DataFrame()

df_pred["Actual Values"] = y_train  # actual values
df_pred["Fitted Values"] = olsmodel2.fittedvalues  # predicted values
df_pred["Residuals"] = olsmodel2.resid  # residuals

df_pred.head()
Out[ ]:
Actual Values Fitted Values Residuals
1744 4.261975 4.306455 -0.044479
3141 4.175156 3.823123 0.352033
1233 4.117410 4.437012 -0.319602
3046 3.782597 3.839788 -0.057191
2649 3.981922 3.935875 0.046047
In [ ]:
# let's plot the fitted values vs residuals

sns.residplot(
    data=df_pred, x="Fitted Values", y="Residuals", color="purple", lowess=True
)
plt.xlabel("Fitted Values")
plt.ylabel("Residuals")
plt.title("Fitted vs Residual plot")
plt.show()

TEST FOR NORMALITY¶

  • I will test for normality by checking the distribution of residuals, by checking the Q-Q plot of residuals, and by using the Shapiro-Wilk test.
  • If the residuals follow a normal distribution, they will make a straight line plot, otherwise not.
  • If the p-value of the Shapiro-Wilk test is greater than 0.05, we can say the residuals are normally distributed.
In [ ]:
sns.histplot(data=df_pred, x='Residuals');
plt.title("Normality of residuals")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
import pylab
import scipy.stats as stats

stats.probplot(df_pred['Residuals'], dist="norm", plot=pylab)
plt.show()
In [ ]:
stats.shapiro(df_pred['Residuals'])
Out[ ]:
ShapiroResult(statistic=np.float64(0.9641212173261121), pvalue=np.float64(4.738535611322251e-24))

TEST FOR HOMOSCEDASTICITY¶

  • I will test for homoscedasticity using the goldfeldquandt test.
  • If we get a p-value greater than 0.05, I will say that the residuals are homoscedastic. Otherwise, they are heteroscedastic.
In [ ]:
import statsmodels.stats.api as sms
from statsmodels.compat import lzip

name = ["F statistic", "p-value"]
test = sms.het_goldfeldquandt(df_pred["Residuals"], x_train3)
lzip(name, test)
Out[ ]:
[('F statistic', np.float64(0.9244081193962604)),
 ('p-value', np.float64(0.9130999803144949))]

Final Model¶

In [95]:
olsmodel_final = sm.OLS(y_train, x_train3).fit()
print(olsmodel_final.summary())

Out[97]:
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
0 0.241178 0.190888 0.829191 0.827526 4.568832

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

Actionable Insights¶

Based on an in-depth analysis and the Linear Regression model, these are some actionable insights for ReCell to develop a comprehensive pricing strategy for used and refurbished devices:

  • With a substantial positive coefficient, by far, the normalized_new_price is the strongest predictor of normalized_used_price. Based on this intuition, expensive new devices, however, retain higher usage values.

  • The age_of_model (derived from release_year) shows that older devices get lower prices, with a significant negative coefficient confirming it.

  • The screen_size, selfie_camera_mp, main_camera_mp, ram, int_memory, and weight have significant positive coefficients. This shows that devices with better/higher specifications command higher used prices.

  • It is counter-intuitive for a desirable feature based on the battery coefficient that was slightly negative.

  • Whereas many brand_name dummy variables were not significant individually , brand_name_Celkon notably had a negative coefficient, and 4g_yes had a positive one. os_iOS and brand_name_Apple showed high multicollinearity, but Apple devices are generally premium.

  • The days_used features were not a statistically significant predictor in this model.

  • Dynamic trends can shift based on the nature of the used device market because the model is built on historical data up to 2021.

  • The residuals were not perfectly normally distributed.

By implementing these data-driven insights, ReCell can develop a more accurate, robust, and dynamic pricing strategy that aligns with market realities and maximizes profitability in the used and refurbished device market.

Actionable Recommendations¶

Based on an in-depth analysis and the Linear Regression model, these are some actionable recommendations for ReCell to develop a comprehensive pricing strategy for used and refurbished devices:

  • The original new price of a device should be used as the baseline for its used pricing algorithm, as the baseline for its used pricing algorithm and the dynamic pricing model must weigh heavily on this factor.

  • Implement depreciation curve that is based on the device's age. However, newer models will be priced higher, while older models face a consistent price reduction. Inventory management helps in prioritizing quicker sale of older stock.

  • When evaluating purchase or sale for devices, ReCell should focus on these specifications in its pricing models. Devices with larger screens, more RAM, higher megapixel cameras, and greater internal storage should get premium priced. For marketing, these highlighted features can be justifiable prices to customers.

  • Investigate further this relationship . It might show that older devices are associated with very large batteries, or larger tablets that might have a demand profile differently. If the negative correlation persists, ReCell should not price higher for larger batteries and instead consider the device type and age.

  • Brands like Celkon, expectations for lower pricing should be set. As a significant factor, 4G-enabled devices are priced competitively. For marketing, these features can be highlighted to justify prices to customers. For premium brands like Apple, they generally command higher prices despite multicollinearity with iOS. For more nuanced pricing strategies, place more emphasis on market segmentation by brand and operating system.

  • While it's a proxy for wear and tear, other factors like initial new price and age of model seem to capture the majority of price depreciation. ReCell can focus more on the age of the model and the device condition and less on the exact days_used which is better reflected in other aspects of a used device's value.

  • Periodically, ReCell should retrain the model with the latest data and add new features (e.g., grade of refurbishment and condition) as they become available. Adoption of 5G(not currently significant, but growing) could become important in the future.

  • For future iterations, explore the transformations of the normalized_used_price (e.g., logarithmic) or alternative regression models (e.g., tree-based models) which are less sensitive to this assumption, if higher prediction accuracy is required or if the current model struggles with specific price ranges.
